title: HTML — The Skeleton of Every Web Page
description: Learn the 10 HTML tags you'll use 90% of the time. Build a real page, not a toy.
learningObjectives: I can explain what HTML is and what it does
I can use the 10 most important HTML tags
I can build a basic web page with text, links, and images

contentSections.body: Every website you've ever visited — from Google to your favorite blog — is built on HTML. It's been around since 1991 and it's not going anywhere. HTML is the skeleton. CSS is the skin. JavaScript is the muscles. Start with the skeleton.

**HTML** (HyperText Markup Language) describes the *structure* of a web page using tags. A tag looks like this: `content` Every HTML file has the same basic structure: ```html My Page

Hello, world!

``` - `` — metadata, page title, links to CSS (not visible) - `` — everything the user sees

contentSections.question: What's the difference between `<head>` and `<body>` in an HTML document?
contentSections.answer: `<head>` contains metadata about the page (title, CSS links, charset) — invisible to users. `<body>` contains the actual visible content — text, images, buttons, everything users see and interact with.
contentSections.hint: Think about what a user can see vs. what the browser reads but doesn't show

contentSections.body: HTML is structure, not design. You're telling the browser *what* things are (heading, paragraph, link), not how they look.

contentSections.title: The 10 tags you'll use every day

contentSections.body:
You don't need to memorize all HTML tags. These 10 cover 90% of real use: ```html

Main title

Section title

A paragraph of text.

Bold text Italic text Click here
  • Item 1
  • Item 2
A container for grouping elements
``` **Always include `alt` on images** — it's required for accessibility and SEO.

**Ask Claude to scaffold HTML for you.** 'Write an HTML page with a header, a main section with 3 feature cards, and a footer. Use semantic HTML.' Claude will generate the structure; you customize the content.

contentSections.question: Why does every `` tag need an `alt` attribute?
contentSections.answer: Screen readers (used by blind and low-vision users) read the alt text aloud. Search engines also use alt text to understand images. Missing alt = inaccessible page. Empty alt (`alt=""`) is valid for decorative images — it tells screen readers to skip it.
contentSections.hint: Think about users who can't see the image — how do they know what it shows?

contentSections.body: Learn 10 tags deeply, not 100 tags shallowly. h1-h2, p, a, img, ul/li, div, button — that's 90% of real HTML.

contentSections.title: Build your first page — right now

contentSections.body:
Create a file called `index.html` and open it in your browser. Build a personal intro page: ```html Sarah Chen — Portfolio

Sarah Chen

Freelance designer learning to code from bed. Chronic illness warrior.

What I'm building

  • Design automation tools
  • Accessibility-first web apps

Get in touch

Email me

``` Open `index.html` in Chrome. You have a website. It's ugly — CSS comes next — but it *works*.

title: CSS — Make It Look Good
description: CSS turns your HTML skeleton into something beautiful. Colors, fonts, spacing, layout — all CSS.
learningObjectives: I can write CSS selectors to target HTML elements
I can apply colors, fonts, and spacing with CSS
I can use Flexbox to create simple layouts

contentSections.body: Right now your HTML page looks like a Word document from 1995. CSS is what makes it look like a real website. Same HTML content, completely different appearance — all controlled by CSS.

contentSections.question: Why should you use classes instead of IDs for most styling?
contentSections.answer: IDs must be unique per page — you can only use an ID once. Classes can be reused on multiple elements. If you have 6 cards, you use `class='card'` on all 6. `id='card'` would only work on one.
contentSections.hint: Think about what 'ID' means — can two people have the same ID?

contentSections.body: CSS selects elements and applies styles. Tag selectors are broad, class selectors are reusable, ID selectors are unique.

contentSections.title: Colors, fonts, and the box model

contentSections.question: You have a button with `padding: 12px 24px`. What does this mean?
contentSections.answer: 12px top and bottom (vertical), 24px left and right (horizontal). Shorthand: `padding: [top/bottom] [left/right]`. For all four sides: `padding: 12px 24px 12px 24px` (top right bottom left — clockwise).

contentSections.body: Padding = space inside the element. Margin = space outside. When layout looks wrong, check these first.

title: JavaScript in the Browser — DOM Basics
description: Your JavaScript can change the page. Click a button, update the content. This is interactivity.
learningObjectives: I can select HTML elements with JavaScript
I can respond to user clicks with event listeners
I can update page content dynamically with JavaScript

contentSections.body:
When you write `

Hello

` in HTML, the browser turns it into a JavaScript object you can access and modify. Change the object, the page updates instantly. This is the DOM — and it's how every interactive website works.

contentSections.question: What's the difference between `querySelector` and `querySelectorAll`?
contentSections.answer: `querySelector` returns the *first* matching element (or null if none found). `querySelectorAll` returns *all* matching elements as a NodeList (like an array). Use `querySelector` when you want one element, `querySelectorAll` when you want to work with multiple.

contentSections.body: The DOM is your bridge between JavaScript and HTML. Select an element, change its properties, the page updates instantly.

**Event listener gotcha:** Make sure the HTML element exists before you add a listener. If your JS runs before the HTML loads, `querySelector` returns `null` and `null.addEventListener()` throws an error. Wrap your code in `document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () => { ... })` or put your `<script>` tag at the bottom of `<body>`.

contentSections.question: What does `event.target` refer to inside an event listener callback?
contentSections.answer: `event.target` is the specific element that triggered the event. For a click listener on a button, `event.target` is that button element. For an input listener, `event.target.value` gives you what the user typed.
contentSections.hint: Think about the arrow pointing at 'target' — what did the user actually interact with?

contentSections.body: `.addEventListener('event', callback)` is how your code listens for user actions. `event.target` is the element they interacted with.

contentSections.title: Build a counter — your first interactive app

title: Forms & User Input
description: Get data from users. Forms, validation, and handling what users type — essential for any real app.
learningObjectives: I can build an HTML form and access its values with JavaScript
I can validate user input before processing it
I can prevent a form from reloading the page

contentSections.body: Forms are everywhere: login pages, checkout flows, contact forms, settings pages. If a user needs to give you data, you need a form. Master forms and you can build the input layer for any app.

The core form elements:

```html
<form id="signup-form">
  <!-- Text input -->
  <label for="name">Your name</label>
  <input type="text" id="name" name="name" placeholder="Sarah" required>

  <!-- Email input -->
  <label for="email">Email</label>
  <input type="email" id="email" name="email" required>

  <!-- Password -->
  <input type="password" id="password" minlength="8">

  <!-- Checkbox -->
  <input type="checkbox" id="terms" name="terms">
  <label for="terms">I agree to the terms</label>

  <!-- Dropdown -->
  <select id="plan" name="plan">
    <option value="free">Free</option>
    <option value="pro">Pro — €12/month</option>
  </select>

  <button type="submit">Sign up</button>
</form>
```

**Always pair `<label>` with `<input>` using `for`/`id`** — required for accessibility.

contentSections.question: Why should you always pair `<label>` with `<input>`?
contentSections.answer: Screen readers announce the label when the input gets focus — without it, users don't know what the field is for. Also, clicking a label focuses the associated input, improving usability (especially for checkboxes which are hard to click precisely).
contentSections.hint: Think about screen readers and what happens when you click a label

contentSections.body: Always pair labels with inputs using for/id. Use `required`, `type`, and `minlength` for built-in HTML validation.

contentSections.question: What happens if you forget `event.preventDefault()` in your form submit handler?
contentSections.answer: The browser submits the form the old-fashioned way: the page reloads, query params appear in the URL (for GET forms), and your JavaScript state is lost. Your `console.log` will appear for a split second, then the page refreshes.
contentSections.hint: What does a form do by default when submitted?

contentSections.body: `event.preventDefault()` prevents page reload. Always call it first in form submit handlers.
**Client-side validation is UX, not security.** It's easily bypassed. Always validate again on the server. Never trust data coming from the browser.

title: Deploy to Vercel — 5 Minutes to Live
description: Get your app on the internet. Vercel makes deployment stupidly easy. Go from local file to live URL.
learningObjectives: I can push my project to GitHub
I can deploy a static site to Vercel in under 5 minutes
I can understand the difference between a local and a deployed app

contentSections.body: An app on your laptop is just a file. An app on the internet is a product. The difference between a hobbyist and a developer is shipping. Today you ship.

**What deployment means:**

Right now, your `index.html` lives on your laptop. Only you can see it (by opening the file). Deployment means uploading it to a server that's always on, so *anyone with a URL* can see it.

**Three deployment layers:**
1. **Your code** → uploaded to GitHub (version control + source)
2. **Vercel** → reads your GitHub repo, hosts the files on their servers
3. **Users** → visit your Vercel URL, get your files served from a nearby server

**Vercel** is free for personal projects. It auto-deploys every time you push to GitHub. No server config required.

contentSections.question: Why is it better to deploy from GitHub instead of uploading files directly to a server?
contentSections.answer: GitHub keeps your history — you can roll back if something breaks. Vercel watches your GitHub repo and auto-deploys on every push, so you never manually upload files. It also lets collaborators contribute without server access.
contentSections.hint: Think about what happens when a deployment breaks — how do you recover?

contentSections.body: GitHub = source of truth. Vercel = automatic deployment from GitHub. Push to GitHub → live in 2 minutes.

contentSections.title: Git basics — the 3 commands you need

contentSections.body:
You don't need to master Git to deploy. Learn these 5 commands:

```bash
# 1. Initialize a Git repo in your project
git init

# 2. Stage all your files
git add .

# 3. Commit (save a snapshot)
git commit -m "Initial commit — personal portfolio page"

# 4. Connect to GitHub (after creating a repo on github.com)
git remote add origin https://github.com/yourusername/my-portfolio.git

# 5. Push to GitHub
git push -u origin main
```

You'll run `git add . && git commit -m 'description' && git push` every time you make changes you want to deploy.

If you get confused by git, ask Claude: 'I'm getting this git error: [paste error]. What does it mean?'

contentSections.question: What does `git commit -m 'Fix typo in nav'` do?
contentSections.answer: It saves a snapshot of your staged files with the message 'Fix typo in nav'. Like saving a checkpoint in a video game — you can return to any commit later. The `-m` flag lets you write the message inline.
contentSections.hint: Think of git commits as save points — what does this save point do?

contentSections.body: Git: add → commit → push. Three commands to version your code and push to GitHub.

contentSections.title: Deploy on Vercel — step by step

contentSections.body:
Once your code is on GitHub:

1. Go to **vercel.com** → Sign up with GitHub
2. Click **'Add New Project'**
3. Find your GitHub repo in the list → click **Import**
4. Vercel auto-detects your project type
5. Click **Deploy** — takes about 60 seconds
6. You get a URL like `my-portfolio-abc123.vercel.app`

**Your site is live.**

Every future `git push` to your main branch auto-deploys a new version.

**Custom domain (optional):**
- Buy a domain on Porkbun (~€10/year)
- In Vercel → Domains → Add `yourdomain.com`
- Add the DNS records Vercel tells you to
- Wait 5 minutes → `yourdomain.com` points to your app
**Vercel free tier covers:** Unlimited personal projects, 100GB bandwidth/month, automatic HTTPS, global CDN. You will not need to pay for hosting unless you're getting thousands of daily users.
